coredata.dc:title: 12.35% efficient graphene quantum dots/silicon heterojunction solar cells using graphene transparent electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Diao, Senlin
Zhang, Xiujuan
Shao, Zhibin
Ding, Ke
Jie, Jiansheng
Zhang, Xiaohong
coredata.dc:description: Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have lately intrigued intensive interest because of their great promise in energy, optoelectronic, and bio-imaging applications. Herein, we demonstrated the fabrication of highly efficient GQDs/n-silicon heterojunction solar cells via a simple solution process. Owing to the unique band structure, the GQDs layer could not only serve as hole transport layer to facilitate the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, but also act as electron blocking layer to suppress the carrier recombination at anode. Moreover, graphene was used as the transparent top electrode for the heterojunction solar cells, ensuring the efficient light absorption and carrier collection. By adjusting the sizes of GQDs and the thickness of GQDs layer, a power conversion efficiency (PCE) as high as 12.35% under AM 1.5G irradiation was achieved, which represented a new efficiency record for this new-type solar cell. The devices also exhibited excellent stability in air due to the high chemical/physical stability of GQDs and graphene. The successful achievement of the high-efficiency GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells opens up the opportunities for their potential applications in high-performance and low-cost photovoltaics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Silicon
Solar cell
Graphene
Heterojunction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of the GQDs. (a) TEM image and (b) SAED pattern of the GQDs. (c), (d) HRTEM images of a GQD and the corresponding FFT image. (e), (f) Two-dimensional HRTEM image of a GQD and the corresponding FFT image. (g) PL spectra measured under different excitation wavelengths. (h) UV–vis absorption spectrum. (i) Photograph of the aqueous solution of GQDs.

(a) Schematic illustration of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cell with graphene film on top as the transparent electrode. J-V curves of the GQDs/Si solar cell measured (b) in the dark, and (c) under AM 1.5G light irradiation at 100mWcm−2. Device counterparts without GQDs layer were also measured for comparison. The inset shows the photograph of a GQDs/Si heterojunction device. (d) Energy band diagram of the GQDs/Si heterojunction device with graphene transparent electrode. Δ Ec represents the offset between the Ec of Si and LUMO of GQDs.

(a) Spatial mapping of the minority carrier lifetime of sample without GQDs. (b)–(d) Spatial mapping of the minority carrier lifetimes of samples after drop-casting different volumes of GQDs aqueous solution of 300, 900, and 1500μL, respectively.

(a), (b), (c) show TEM images of GQDs with various sizes. Insets show the corresponding size distributions. (d) J-V curves and (e) EQE spectra of GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells fabricated using GQDs with different sizes. (f) J-V curves of the devices after performing graphene doping. Devices without GQDs layer were also measured for comparsion.

(a), (b), (c) AFM images of GQDs layer with various thickness of 8, 20, 50nm, respectively. (d) J-V curves and (e) J-V curves of the devices with various GQDs layer thickness after performing HNO3 doped on graphene films. (f) EQE spectra of the 2–6nm GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells with different GQDs layer thickness.
Variations of (a) V oc/J sc, and (b) FF/PCE of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cell over time.
Summary of the photovoltaic parameters of the solar cells studied in this work.

head.ce:title: 12.35% efficient graphene quantum dots/silicon heterojunction solar cells using graphene transparent electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology (Nano-CIC), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science and Technology (Nano-CIC), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have lately intrigued intensive interest because of their great promise in energy, optoelectronic, and bio-imaging applications. Herein, we demonstrated the fabrication of highly efficient GQDs/n-silicon heterojunction solar cells via a simple solution process. Owing to the unique band structure, the GQDs layer could not only serve as hole transport layer to facilitate the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, but also act as electron blocking layer to suppress the carrier recombination at anode. Moreover, graphene was used as the transparent top electrode for the heterojunction solar cells, ensuring the efficient light absorption and carrier collection. By adjusting the sizes of GQDs and the thickness of GQDs layer, a power conversion efficiency (PCE) as high as 12.35% under AM 1.5G irradiation was achieved, which represented a new efficiency record for this new-type solar cell. The devices also exhibited excellent stability in air due to the high chemical/physical stability of GQDs and graphene. The successful achievement of the high-efficiency GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells opens up the opportunities for their potential applications in high-performance and low-cost photovoltaics.

ce:section.ce:para: To harvest the clean renewable solar radiation energy, solar cells are in high demand. Nowadays, the majority of commercial solar cells are still dominated by crystalline silicon (Si) due to its abundant material resources, nontoxic nature, high efficiency, and long-term stability [1]. However, in spite of the rapid decrease of module price in the last few years [2], silicon solar cells are still relatively expensive due to high-temperature and complex manufacturing processes, releasing a big challenge for wide applications [3]. Recently, organic/silicon hybrid solar cells have attracted a great deal of research interest owing to their promise of room-temperature manufacturing, and solution processing capability [4,5]. However, further improvement of air stability is requisite to promote their practical applications [6]. Besides the silicon-based solar cells, quantum dots (QDs), e.g.

CdSe, CdTe, and PbS, based solar cells are emerging due to their size-tunable optical response, efficient multiple carrier generation, and the possibility of boosting the energy conversion efficiency beyond the traditional Shockley-Queisser limit of 32% of Si solar cells [7–9]. Nevertheless, the relatively poor mobilities (10−3–1cm2/Vs) of traditional semiconductor QDs hinder the improvement of device performance. A power conversion efficiency (PCE) of lower than 10% is reported for the QDs-based solar cells [10,11]. In addition to the conductivity, the toxicity of the traditional semiconductor QDs is a concern, too.

Owing to the extraordinary properties like high optical transmittance, low sheet resistance, tunable work function, and excellent chemical/physical stability [12,13], graphene has been widely used as transparent conductive electrodes in solar cells, light-emitting diodes (LEDs), touch panels, etc [14]. Meanwhile, in contrast to the zero bandgap of graphene, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess a tunable bandgap due to the quantum confinement and edge effects [15]. The GQDs show unique optical and electrical transport properties such as excitation wavelength-dependent emission and high conductivity [16,17]. The lifetimes for hot carriers in GQDs are relatively longer, giving rise to the eﬃcient hot-carrier harvesting and multiple carrier generation [18,19]. GQDs can be synthesized in a large quantity through the solution methods, such as hydrothermal cutting of graphene sheets [20] and acidic exfoliation of pitch carbon fibers [21], ensuring the large-scale, cost-effective applications of GQDs. More importantly, the GQDs are nontoxic and highly stable in air. These characteristics make GQDs promising candidates for energy applications [22]. For instance, Chao et al. used GQDs coated VO2 arrays as highly durable electrodes in Li and Na ion batteries to further boost the high-rate capability and long-term cycling stability of the batteries [23]. Gupta et al. have demonstrated that GQDs blended regioregular poly(3-hexylthiophene-2,5-diyl) or poly(2methoxy-5-(2-ethylhexyloxy)-1,4phenylenevinylene) polymers could result in a significant improvement in the efficiency of organic photovoltaic (OPV) devices [24]. Li et al. also applied functional GQDs as electron-acceptor materials in conjugated polymer photovoltaic devices, and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 1.28% was achieved [25]. We first proposed a new type of heterojunction solar cells, i.e., GQDs/n-Si heterojunction solar cells by coating a layer of GQDs on n-Si substrate [26]. However, the device only exhibited moderate efficiency of 6.63% due to the use of thick Au top electrode, which prevented the efficient light absorption of the solar cells.

Herein, we further demonstrated a highly efficient GQDs/Si solar cell via a simple solution approach using graphene as transparent electrode. By virtue of the formation of GQDs/Si heterojunction, the photo-generated electron-hole pairs could be effectively separated at GQDs and Si interface. Thanks to the unique electrical transfer properties and band structures of GQDs, the GQDs layer could serve as both hole transport layer and electron blocking layer in the device. Furthermore, graphene film was used as top transparent electrode, ensuring the efficient light absorption and carrier transportation. Through carefully controlling the sizes of GQDs as well as the thickness of GQDs layer, a recorded PCE of 12.35% under AM 1.5G irradiation was achieved for the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells. Significantly, the devices exhibited excellent stability with little degradation after storing in ambient conditions for half a year due to the high stability of GQDs and graphene. Given the simple and cost-effective solution processes, our work unveils the great potential of GQDs for high performance, low-cost solar cells.

Synthesis of GQDs in this study is conducted through a hydrothermal method developed by Pan and co-workers [20]. Graphene oxide (GO) sheets were first obtained via improved Hummers’ method [42]. The GO sheets were thermal deoxidized in a tube furnace at 250°C for 2h with a heating rate of 5°C min−1 in a nitrogen atmosphere to obtain graphene sheets (GSs). A mixed solution of H2SO4 (10mL) and HNO3 (30mL) was used to oxidize GSs (0.05g) under mild ultrasonication (150W, 59kHz). GQDs with different sizes of 6–12, 5–8, and 3–6nm could be fabricated by adjusting the ultrasonication times of 15, 22, and 26h, respectively. The mixture was then diluted with DI water (200mL) and filtered through a 0.15µm microporous membrane to remove the residual acids. Puriﬁed oxidized GSs (0.1g) were redispersed in DI water (40mL) and the pH was tuned to 8 with NaOH. The suspension was transferred to a poly (tetraﬂuoroethylene) (Teflon) lined autoclave (50mL) and heated at 200°C for 12h. After the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the black suspension was filtered through a 0.15µm microporous membrane. Thus, a brown solution was obtained. Eventually, GQDs with various sizes were obtained by dialyzing the colloidal solution in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 3500Da) overnight.

Monolayer graphene (MLG) films were prepared via a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method by using a mixed gas of CH4 (40 sccm) and H2 (20 sccm) at 1000 °C with 25µm-thick Cu foil as the catalytic substrates [43]. 6wt% polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) was spin-coated on the as-prepared graphene films, and then the underlying Cu foils were etched off in Marble’s reagent solution (CuSO4:HCl: H2O=10g:50mL:50mL). The resulted PMMA/MLG film was cleaned in DI water several times for use.

N-type Si substrate (orientation <100>, resistivity 1–3Ωcm−1) with a 300nm thick SiO2 oxidation layer was used in our study. An opening window of 3×3mm2 was first defined on the SiO2/Si substrate by an adhesive tape, which defined the effective work area of the solar cell. The SiO2 insulative layer within the window was removed by buffered oxide etch (BOE) solution to expose the Si surface, followed by rinsing in deionized (DI) water for 5min. Afterwards, the substrate was dried by nitrogen gas stream and the adhesive tapes on the substrate were peeled off. Surface methylation modification on the exposed Si surface within the window was conducted following a two-step chlorinaion/alkylation method [44]. First, the SiO2/Si substrate was dipped into a saturated PCl5 solution in chlorobenzene (CB) for 2h at 140°C, then the substrate was transferred into CH3MgCl/tetrahydrofuran (THF) (1:2) solution for 14h at 70 °C. By this method, the dangling bonds on the Si surface could be passivated by the methyl groups.

To construct the GQDs/Si solar cells, Ti/Au (2nm/50nm) electrode was first deposited on the SiO2 layer nearby the exposed Si window by e-beam evaporation with a shadow mask. Then the aqueous solution of GQDs was dropped into the opening window on the CH3-terminated Si surface. GQDs layer with approximate thickness of 8, 20, 50nm was obtained after applying 120, 400, 640μL GQDs solution, respectively. To obtain five-layer graphene (FLG) films, the PMMA-supported MLG film was directly transferred onto another Cu foil that has CVD fabricated graphene on it. After dried at 120°C for 5min, the PMMA-supported FLG film was placed in Marble’s reagent solution to remove the underneath Cu foil. This process was repeated four times to obtain FLG films (Fig. S4). Fig. S5 depicts the AFM images of as-prepared MLG and FLG films, revealing a surface roughness of 0.49 and 3.98nm. In addition, Table S1 and Fig. S6 show the square resistance and the optical transmittance of graphene films with different layers, respectively. The FLG film possesses a small square resistance of 0.29kΩ/□ and a high transmittance of 88–90% in 500–1100nm, verifying the high quality of the graphene films [45,46]. Then the FLG films was transferred onto the top of the GQDs/Si device and dried at 120°C for 5min. The residual PMMA on the FLG films was removed by immersing the device in acetone for 20min. Indium-gallium (In-Ga) alloy was pasted on the rear side of the Si substrate to form the ohmic contact.

Morphologies and structures of GQDs were investigated by TEM (FEI Tecnai GZ F20 S-TWIN). PL and UV–vis spectra were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Flworomax-4) and UV/vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 750), respectively. Band structures of the GQDs were determined by UPS (UPS, Kratos AXIS UltraDLD), which was also used to determine the work function of graphene film. Morphologies of GQDs layer were characterized by AFM (Veeco, MultiMode V). Raman (Jobin Yvon/LabRAM HR800) with 514nm laser excitation was used to characterize the quality of MLG film. Photovoltaic characteristics of the GQDs/Si solar cells were evaluated by a Keithley 2612 source meter in an ambient environment. The minority carrier lifetime was measured by a MDPmap flexible mapping tool (MDPmap, Freiberg Instruments). Newport 91160 solar simulator equipped with a 300W xenon lamp and an air mass (AM) 1.5 filter was used to generate simulated AM 1.5G solar irradiation. The irradiation intensity that calibrated by a Newport standard Si solar cell 91150 was 100mWcm−2. Fig. S8 shows the setup for the device measurements, along with a screenshot from the testing system during testing a GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cell.

GQDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method [20]. Fig. 1(a) displays the low-magnification transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the GQDs, revealing the diameter of 3–6nm for the dots. Selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) are used to further characterize the crystal structures of GQDs. SAED analysis in Fig. 1(b) shows the lattice spacing of 0.21 (yellow rings), 0.18 (red rings), 0.12 (green rings), and 0.10nm (pink rings) for the GQDs, corresponding to the
(
1
̅
100
)
, (
1
̅
102
), (
2
̅
112
), and (
2
̅
114 )
planes of graphite (JCPDF card no. 01-0646), respectively. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1(c) discloses that the GQDs are highly crystallized with a lattice spacing of 0.21nm, which is consistent with the (
1
1
¯
00
) plane of graphite [27,28], as is also confirmed by fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern in Fig. 1(d). Occasionally, two-dimensional crystalline lattice of the GQD could be observed (Fig. 1(e)). The intact lattice, along with the constructed inverse FFT pattern (Fig. 1(f)), further verifies the high quality of the GQDs obtained by the hydrothermal method. Fig. 1(g) shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the aqueous solution of 3–6nm size GQDs. A broad emission peak at 460nm is obtained upon 340nm excitation. The PL peak tends to red-shift from 460 to 565nm when the excitation wavelengths increase from 340 to 500nm (Fig. 1(g)), showing the excitation wavelength-dependent PL emission. This phenomenon has been commonly observed in GQDs and fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials [25,29]. It is suggested that emissive traps [30], electronic conjugate structures [25,29], and the inhomogeneous orbital energy distribution of the defect states are responsible for this phenomenon [30,31]. Fig. 1(h) depicts the UV–vis absorption spectrum of 3–6nm size GQDs. The optical band-gap of the GQDs is estimated to be ca. 3.60eV by taking the intercept of the extrapolation of the absorption edge to x axis (~344nm). From the photograph of the GQDs aqueous solution (0.05wt%, Fig. 1(i)), one can see that the solution is nearly transparent with light yellow color.

Fig. 2(a) shows the schematic illustration of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cell with graphene transparent electrode, and the photograph of a real GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cell is shown in the inset of Fig. 2(c). Raman spectrum of the graphene film, Fig. S1 (Supporting Information), demonstrates a high I 2D/I G intensity ratio of ∼2.84 and a weak D-band scattering, revealing the high crystal quality of the film [32]. Notably, the GQDs/Si device exhibits an excellent rectifying behavior with a rectification ratio of ~104 at ±0.8 V in the dark by virtue of the formation of a heterojunction between GQDs and Si (Fig. 2(b)). The diode performance of GQDs/Si heterojunction is much better than the device counterpart without GQDs, i.e. graphene/Si heterojunction. The smaller saturation current density (J 0) in the GQDs/Si device manifests a lower carrier recombination in the device. Under AM 1.5G illumination, Fig. 2(c), the GQDs/Si heterojunction also exhibits superior photovoltaic characteristics compared to graphene/Si heterojunction, with an open-circuit voltage (V OC), a short-circuit current density (J SC), and a ﬁll factor (FF) of 0.53V, 26.99mAcm−2, and 0.63, respectively, yielding a PCE of 8.95%. In contrast, the PCE is only 4.24% for the graphene/Si device. To understand the role of GQDs in improving the device performance, Fig. 2(d) illustrates the energy band diagram of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells. The work function of pristine graphene is ~4.66eV, which is determined by the ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) spectrum (Fig. S1(b), Supporting Information). The conduction band minimum (E c) and valence band maximum (E v) of n-type Si are 4.05eV and 5.17eV, respectively. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of GQDs is measured by UPS to be 6.56eV for the 3–6nm GQDs. Considering the optical bandgap of ca. 3.60eV for the GQDs, the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) is estimated to be ca. 2.96eV (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). Upon irradiation, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs in Si will diffuse to GQDs/Si interface and then be separated by the built-in electric field of the heterojunction. The holes will transfer through the GQDs layer and then be collected by the graphene electrode (anode), whereas electrons are preferentially collected by the bottom In/Ga electrode (cathode). The use of graphene can ensure the maximum absorption of the incident light due to its high optical transmittance. It is noted that the LUMO position of GQDs is much higher than the E c of Si, such large E c-LUMO offset (ΔΦ, ca. 1.09eV) will prevent electrons from transferring from Si to graphene, thus leading to minimize recombination on the graphene anode side. As for the hole transport, it seems that the E v-HOMO offset will also present an unfavorable barrier for hole transport from Si to GQDs layer. However, this assumption obviously conflicts with the experiment results, which have already revealed the effective hole transport of the GQDs layer (Fig. 2(c)). Therefore, the energy barrier for hole transport should not be as large as that shown in Fig. 2(d). The following reasons could be resposible for the effective hole transport in GQDs layer: (i) The holes can transfer through the GQDs layer via a tunneling process, rather than transfer from the HOMO level [33]. The high p-type conductivity of GQDs in air might be an important factor that contributes to the tunneling process. Fig. S3 in Supporting Information demonstrated that the conductivity of GQDs layer is greatly enhanced in air as compared to that in vacuum, because of the adsorption of oxygen molecules and moisture [34,35]. (ii) The existence of intermediate energy levels among the bandgap of GQDs is another factor that may contribute to the effective hole transport (dash lines in energy band of GQDs in Fig. 2(d)). Theses intermediate energy levels are likely produced by the surface or edge defects/bonds, allowing the transfort of holes through them to graphene. From Fig. 2(c), the control device without GQDs exhibits much lower J sc, V oc, and FF, because the photo-generated electrons have a large chance to transfer to graphene electrode and then recombine with holes at graphene/Si interface, thus leading to a lower PCE. Based on above discussion, we can conclude that the GQDs layer can not only serve as an electron-blocking layer for reducing the carrier recombination, but also act as a hole transport layer in the GQDs/Si device.

To understand the role of GQDs layer in reducing the carrier recombination, a spatial mapping of minority carrier lifetime based on a microwave detected photoconductivity (SteadyState MDP) technique was performed. The minority carrier lifetime of silicon-based solar cells depend on both of bulk and surface recombination, which can be expressed by the following simplified equation [36]:
1
τ
eff
=
1
τ
bulk
+
2
S
w
where
τ
eff
is the effective minority lifetime,
τ
bulk
is the bulk minority lifetime,
S
is the surface recombination rate, and
w
is the substrate thickness. Since we adopted the same silicon substrate during the measurements, their bulk lifetimes will be fixed and therefore the measured lifetimes reflect the surface lifetime differences. We respectively measured the lifetimes of the contrastive planar silicon and the silicon substrates covered with different thickness of GQDs layers, which is controlled by drop-casting different volumes of GQDs aqueous solution. From Fig. 3, the pristine planar silicon has an average lifetime of 19μs, while after drop-casting GQDs all samples exhibit an obvious improvement on minority carrier lifetime to 21, 33, and 41μs, respectively. Overall, the increased minority carrier lifetimes reveal that GQDs layer on Si substrates can effectively reduce the recombination on surface. This could be attributed to the strong built-in electric field between GQDs layer and silicon substrate, which can greatly enhance the separation of photo-generated carriers and further reduce the recombination [36].

Next, device performance of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells was further improved by tuning the size and layer thickness of GQDs as well as performing chemical doping on graphene films. It is reported that the GQDs show size-dependent electrical and optical properties [37]. We fabricated GQDs with various sizes by adjusting the ultrasonic times during the synthesis process. Fig. 4(a)–(c) shows the TEM images of GQDs with varied sizes of 3–6, 5–8, and 6–12nm, respectively. Their optical bandgaps are estimated to be ~3.60eV, ~2.93eV, and ~2.27eV, respectively (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). Fig. 4(d) shows the J-V curves of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells fabricated based on the GQDs with varied sizes before HNO3 doping on graphene. The thickness of the GQDs layer was fixed at ca. 20nm for better comparsion. It is found that all the devices show much enhanced photovoltaic perforamnce with repect to the bare Si without GQDs layer. V oc/FF tend to increase with decreasing GQDs size, which are 0.51 V/0.50, 0.55 V/0.54, and 0.56 V/0.63 for 6–12, 5–8, and 3–6nm GQDs, respectively. On the contrary, the J sc decreases with decreasing GQDs size from 32.85mAcm−2 for 6–12nm GQDs to 29.84mAcm−2 for 3–6nm GQDs. Integration on the EQE specra of the devices with different sizes fits well with the J-V curves (Fig. 3(e)). As a result, PCE is deduced to be 8.46%, 9.21%, and 10.50% for 6–12, 5–8, and 3–6nm size GQDs, respectively. It is known that HNO3 doping on graphene has been commonly used to improve its conductivity [38]. Therefore we further expose the devices to HNO3 vapor for 30s to dope the graphene films. V oc, FF, and J sc are remarkably improved upon the graphene doping, resulting in an improved PCE of 9.49%, 10.76%, and 11.43% for 6–12, 5–8, and 3–6nm size GQDs, respectively (Fig. 3(f)). The photovoltaic parameters of the solar cells are summarized in Table 1, indicating that 3–6nm GQDs/Si solar cell possesses the best photovoltaic performance. The GQDs size-dependent performance is a result of the distinct band structures of the GQDs with different sizes. The LUMO levels are deduced to be ~3.74, ~3.14, and ~2.96eV for 6–12, 5–8, and 3–6nm size GQDs by UPS measurements, respectively. Therefore the corresponding barriers for electron transport at graphene side, i.e., E c-LUMO offsets, are 0.31, 0.91, and 1.09eV, respectively. It is clear that 3–6nm GQDs-based device has largest electron barrier and consequently highest V oc and FF. Meanwhile, the HOMO level will also increases with decreasing size of GQDs, resulting in the increased barrier for hole transport. This makes J sc decrease when shrinking GQDs size. However, it seems that the improvement in V oc and FF is more pronounced than the decrease of J sc, eventually leading to a highest efficiency for 3–6nm GQDs-based device.

Besides the GQDs size, thickness of the GQDs layer is another important factor that determines the photovoltaic performance of GQDs/Si heterojunctions. In this work, we fabricated the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells with varied layer thickness by drop-coasting 120, 400, and 640μL GQDs solution on the substrate, respectively. Atomic force microscope (AFM) characterizations in Fig. 5(a)–(c) indicate that the resultant GQDs layers are very rough on the substrates due to the aggregation of GQDs, making it difficult to precisely determine the layer thickness. However, an average layer thickness of 8, 20, 50nm, respectively, could be estimated from the AFM images. The cross-sectional SEM images of a device with 50nm GQDs layer are shown in Fig. S7. The GQDs layer shows good contact with Si substrate, in despite of the large surface roughness of the GQDs layer. Fig. 5(d) depicts the J-V curves of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells with varied layer thickness. The optimal 3–6nm GQDs were used for all the devices. In addition, HNO3 doping on the graphene transparent electrode was conducted to further enhance the device performance (Fig. 5(f)). It is observed that PCE of the device increases remakably from 9.43–12.35% as the GQDs layer thickness increaes from 8nm to 20nm. However, futher increase of the GQDs layer thickness to 50nm results in the degradation of the device performance. Fig. 5(e) shows the corresponding EQE spectra of devices with different GQDs layer thickness, confirming the J-V measurement results. We speculate that the nonuniform GQDs layer might be responsible for the relatviely poor device performance at small layer thickness (8nm); only small portion of the substrate surface is covered by the GQDs, thus graphene can directly contact with Si in most area, resulting in the decrease of V oc, J sc, and FF. The increase of PCE for 20nm GQDs layer can be ascribe to the reduced contact area of graphene with Si [39]. However, the light-blocking effect as well as carrier recombination may be contributed to the degradation of device performance at a large thickness of 50nm. From Fig. S2(a) (Supporting Information), the GQDs have a absorption tail extending to the visile light range. Thick GQDs layer will then absorb more light and reduce the light harvested by Si, causing the optical loss of the solar cell. In addition, the relatively poor electrical conductivity of thick GQDs layer will increase the series resistance in the cell circuit, which aggravates carrier recombination in GQDs layer and degrades the device performance.

Owing to the high chemical/physical stability of GQDs and graphene transparent electrode, the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells are presumed to possess high environmental stability. Therefore, we further investigated the stability of the GQDs/Si solar cell by storing it in air for 6 months without any encapsulation. Fig. 6 depicts the photovoltaic parameters of the GQDs/Si device with respect to the storing time. The cell is not HNO3 doped to eliminate the fluctuation caused by different doping conditions. Obviously, the device can work well after 6 months, though the PCE decreases a little bit from 10.67–9.28%, revealing ~13% degradation of the performance. It is worthly noting that both V oc and FF are nearly unchanged, whereas the J sc decreases from 30.74 to 28.85mAcm−2. The gradual invalidation of CH3 termination over time and the oxidation of Si surface, which will increase the barrier for carrier transport, are the possible reasons for the decrease of J sc. It is expected that the device durability could be further improved by using more stable passivation methods, such as the passivation with ultrathin Al2O3 layer or intrinsic a-Si layer in next step work [40,41].

In conclusion, highly efficent GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells with graphene transparent electrode were demonstrated. The heterojunction exhibited pronounced rectifying behavior with a rectification ratio of ~104 at ±0.8 V. Owing to the unique band structure, the GQDs layer can not only serve as hole transport layer but also play an important role in suppressing the carrier recombination due to the high heterojunction barrier height of 1.09eV. As a result, the efficiency of Si/GQDs heterojunction solar cells were remarkably improved as compared to the device counterparts without GQDs layer. Moreover, it was found that the device performance was also impacted by the sizes of GQDs as well as thickness of the GQDs layer, and a optimal PCE of 12.35% was achieved for 3–6nm size GQDs with layer thickness of ~20nm. To the best of our knowledge, this is the highest efficiency for this new kind solar cell. Moreover, the heterojunction solar cells showed excellent stability due to the high chemical/physical stability of GQDs and graphene. Various potential improvements of the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells can be envisioned including the fabrication of more uniform and smooth GQDs layer on silicon substrate or the use of more efficient approach to dope the graphene layer to reduce the sheet resistance. Given the cost-effective device architecture and outstanding performance, the GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cells will open up new opportunities for future high-performance and low-cost photovoltaic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Basic Research Program of China (No. 2013CB933500), the Major Research Plan of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 91333208), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61422403, 51672108, 51622306, 21673151, 51401138), Qing Lan Project, and a Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2016.11.051.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Senlin Diao is currently a graduatae student in the institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University. He received his B.S. degree in the College of Nano Science & Technology (CNST) Soochow University in 2014. His research mainly focuses on the silicon-based heterojunction solar cells.

Xiujuan Zhang received her Ph. D. degree in chemistry from Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS) in 2006. After that, she worked as a postdoctoral fellow at City University of Hong Kong. In 2009, she joined institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University. Her research involves design and synthesis of semiconductor nanostructure and their applications in optoelectronic devices.

Zhibin Shao received his B. S. and Ph. D. degree in physics from University of Science and Technology of China (USTC) in 2007 and 2013, respectively. After that, he joined institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, as a postdoctoral fellow. His research involves the surface charge transfer doping (SCTD) of low-dimensional nanostructures and their application in opto/electronic nanodevices.

Ke Ding received his B.S. degree from Hefei University of Technology in 2013. He is currently a Ph.D student in Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University. His research interests include nano-electronic and nano-optoelectronic devices, graphene and relevant devices, and solar cells based on silicon nanoarrays.

Jiansheng Jie received his B. S. and Ph. D. degree in physics from University of Science and Technology of China (USTC) in 1999 and 2004 respectively. He then went to Prof. Shuit-tong Lee’s lab as a postdoctoral fellow at City University of Hong Kong. After that, he started to work at Hefei University of Technology as a professor in 2006. In 2011, he joined Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University. His research focuses on semiconductor nanostructures and their applications in flexible electronics, optoelectronics, and solar energy conversion devices.

Xiaohong Zhang received his B. S. and M. S. degree at Beihang University in 1989 and 1992, respectively. He obtained his Ph. D. degree at Beijing Institute of Technology in 1996. After that, he worked as post-doctor at Institute of Photographic Chemistry of Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), and City University of Hong Kong respectively. He joined Technical Institute of Physics and Chemistry of CAS as a professor in 2000. Since 2013, he came to Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, as a professor. His research interests include organic optoelectronic materials, semiconductor nanomaterials and optoelectronic devices.
